stem: Evaluate the limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{x \to [[x0]]} \dfrac{x^{[[n]]} - ([[x0]])^{[[n]]}}{x - [[x0]]} \), which has the indeterminate form \( \frac{0}{0} \).
solution: By L'Hopital's rule, differentiate top and bottom: \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to [[x0]]}\dfrac{[[n]]x^{[[n]]-1}}{1} = [[n]]([[x0]])^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This plugs \( x=[[x0]] \) into the original numerator instead of applying L'Hopital's rule.
This forgets to lower the exponent by one after differentiating.
This differentiates a second time even though the limit was already determinate after one application.
This reports the point of evaluation itself, not the value of the limit.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin([[a]]x)}{\sin([[b]]x)} \), which has the indeterminate form \( \frac{0}{0} \).
solution: By L'Hopital's rule, differentiate top and bottom: \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{[[a]]\cos([[a]]x)}{[[b]]\cos([[b]]x)} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \) (since \( \cos(0)=1 \)).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct ratio \( a/b \).
This multiplies the coefficients instead of taking their ratio.
This squares both coefficients before taking the ratio.
This subtracts the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{[[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]])}{[[d]]x^{2} + ([[e]])x + ([[f]])} \), which has the indeterminate form \( \frac{\infty}{\infty} \).
solution: Applying L'Hopital's rule twice (or dividing every term by \( x^{2} \)): \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}\dfrac{2\cdot[[a]]x+([[b]])}{2\cdot[[d]]x+([[e]])} = \lim_{x\to\infty}\dfrac{2\cdot[[a]]}{2\cdot[[d]]} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This takes the ratio of the linear coefficients instead of the leading quadratic coefficients.
This takes the ratio of the constant terms instead of the leading coefficients.
This inverts the correct ratio of leading coefficients.
This mistakenly assumes a rational function with equal degrees always tends to 0 at infinity.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{e^{[[a]]x} - 1}{\sin([[b]]x)} \), which has the indeterminate form \( \frac{0}{0} \).
solution: By L'Hopital's rule, differentiate top and bottom: \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{[[a]]e^{[[a]]x}}{[[b]]\cos([[b]]x)} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \) (since \( e^{0}=1 \) and \( \cos(0)=1 \)).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct ratio \( a/b \).
This multiplies the rate constants instead of dividing them.
This squares the denominator's constant instead of leaving it linear.
This mistakenly assumes the limit vanishes just because the numerator does at \( x=0 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos([[a]]x)}{x^{2}} \), which has the indeterminate form \( \frac{0}{0} \) (L'Hopital's rule must be applied twice).
solution: Applying L'Hopital's rule once gives \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{[[a]]\sin([[a]]x)}{2x} \), still \( \frac{0}{0} \). Applying it again gives \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{[[a]]^{2}\cos([[a]]x)}{2} = \dfrac{[[a]]^{2}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This stops after only one application of L'Hopital's rule, which is still indeterminate.
This forgets to divide by 2 after the second application.
This divides by an extra factor of 2, undercounting the correct value.
This mistakenly assumes the limit is 0 just because the numerator vanishes at \( x=0 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
